_readme: THIS FILE IS THE ENTIRE SITE'S CONTENT. To add a project: (1) create a folder images/<slug>/ containing cover.jpg plus 01.jpg, 02.jpg, ... (2) copy one of the blocks below, fill it in, and add it to the top of the 'projects' array. Order in this array = order on the homepage.

site.name: Amelia Forman
site.tagline: Interior design student at Parsons School of Design, New York.
site.intro:
**Architecturally Structured. Materially Rich. Quietly Atmospheric.**
*Interiors with formality, comfort, and a cultivated sense of permanence.*
about: Amelia Forman is an interior design student at Parsons School of Design. Her work is grounded in history, materials, and form. She focuses on proportion and restraint, often drawing from historical references to create interiors that feel clear yet warm. She has a strong interest in vintage furniture, using authentic secondhand pieces to create layered, lasting interiors.

She holds a B.A. in Economics and Spanish from Wellesley College, summa cum laude, and is completing an A.A.S. in Interior Design at Parsons, expected May 2026. Her training spans AutoCAD, SketchUp, V-Ray rendering, hand drafting, perspective drawing, and physical model making. She speaks English and Spanish.

projects.title: The New Lobby
projects.description: Reimagines an uninviting institutional lobby as a vibrant community hub rooted in Parsons' interdisciplinary culture and student work. Existing conditions were surveyed and developed into demolition and proposed plan drawings, transforming a rigid space into an interdisciplinary center: visitors grab a coffee, gather in the amphitheater while a student plays the grand piano, and multi-story display walls showcase work across fashion, interiors, and music.

projects.title: Drawing Room
projects.description: A formal drawing room organized around symmetry, axial views, and curated focal elements. An existing floor plan was scaled and developed into a fully designed room: two central vistas guide movement, corner glazing is preserved, full-track curtains regulate light and heat, and furniture and artwork act as sculptural anchors. A Jack Whitten painting anchors the mantel, its tones informing the counter and mantel marble and a custom marble coffee table. Furnishings include a Barcelona daybed, Pierre Paulin ribbon chairs, and a Pierre Jeanneret and Le Corbusier upholstered sofa; an alternate Franco-Japanese scheme substitutes a modular Vuarnesson coffee table and a Charlotte Perriand bed for warmer materials and a softer sense of order.

projects.title: Symmetry Residence
projects.description: Transforms a compact classroom footprint into a functional living space guided by symmetry and negative space, with all drawings completed by hand. Inspired by a visit to the Merchant's House Museum, a classic historic floor plan is reinterpreted in reverse: instead of a wall and doorway separating public and private spaces, a large central built-in defines the division — a kitchen on the public side, a vanity opposite — with negative space surrounding it to establish the primary axis of symmetry. Simple, symmetrical furniture with handmade qualities balances accuracy with a human touch.

images.caption: Floor plan — hand drafted, 3/8" = 1'-0"
Kitchen side of the central built-in — concept sketch, watercolor
Vanity side — concept sketch, watercolor; furniture reference: Jean Royère Ondulation armchair

projects.title: Porcelain Atelier
projects.description: A porcelain gallery inspired by the material's history, shaping an architectural form that functions as both exhibition space and artwork. The building's geometry is derived from a physically broken porcelain plate, allowing the fragments to determine the form. Glossy walls contrast with rough earthenware floors, reinforcing porcelain's value; gold-lined fissures reference kintsugi and carry hidden light, while south-facing glass walls admit shifting daylight that changes how the porcelain is seen over time.
